attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman has just undergone a forceps-assisted vaginal delivery of her first baby. She has sustained a perineal tear involving the vaginal mucosa and perineal muscles, but the anal sphincter complex and rectal mucosa is intact. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 85/min, blood pressure 125/80 mmHg, and estimated blood loss is 300 mL. The perineum is tender but there is no active bleeding.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate location for repair of the perineal tear?
attributes.explanation:
This scenario describes an uncomplicated second-degree perineal tear following forceps delivery. The key features suggesting this can be safely managed in the birth suite include: intact anal sphincter, haemodynamic stability, and moderate blood loss.

Birth suite with local anaesthetic is the most appropriate setting for repair. Second-degree tears involve the vaginal mucosa and perineal muscles but not the anal sphincter complex. With adequate lighting, positioning, and local anaesthetic infiltration, these can be safely and effectively repaired in the birth suite.

Operating theatre with local anaesthetic would be unnecessary for a second-degree tear unless there were complicating factors such as uncontrolled bleeding or difficulty achieving adequate exposure in the birth suite.

Operating theatre with spinal anaesthetic would be indicated for repair of more complex tears (third or fourth-degree) involving the anal sphincter complex, which require careful identification and repair of the sphincter muscles.

Operating theatre with general anaesthetic would be excessive for a second-degree tear repair and expose the patient to unnecessary risks. General anaesthetic would only be considered if regional techniques were contraindicated in a complex tear requiring theatre.

Birth suite without anaesthetic would be inappropriate as adequate pain relief is essential for careful repair and patient comfort. Local anaesthetic allows for proper tissue handling and precise suturing technique.

The repair should be performed as soon as practical after delivery, ideally within 24 hours, to reduce infection risk and promote optimal healing. The procedure requires good lighting, appropriate instruments, and careful attention to anatomical alignment.
learningPoints: Second-degree perineal tears should be repaired within 24 hours of delivery to reduce infection risk and promote optimal healing.
Repair of second-degree tears can be safely performed in the birth suite with adequate lighting, positioning, and local anaesthetic.
Transfer to operating theatre is indicated for third/fourth-degree tears, uncontrolled bleeding, or inadequate pain relief.
references: Ramar CN, Vadakekut ES, Grimes WR. Perineal lacerations. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 [cited 2024 Nov 21]. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK559068/

Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG). The management of third- and fourth-degree perineal tears. Green-top Guideline No. 29 [Internet]. London: RCOG; 2015 [cited 2024 Nov 21]. Available from: https://www.rcog.org.uk/media/5jeb5hzu/gtg-29.pdf
